author: @jeremiahembs5343
text: That's amazing. A perfect blend of new and old technology and a great art and chemistry lesson rolled into one. Good show chap. Repeated exposure of 900 seconds at a time would wear a printer screen quickly but I suspect one could lower the light intensity too which may even result in a sharper image.

author: @francoismukagaga
text:
Robert, you are amazing 😊! After your post i saw a video, copied the link, couldn't find the link anymore.
Still i wonder what you would ponder about it,🤔. But please keep doing what you do...even change the music of your shorts,😊. After your printing i saw..."Biotechnology in the Futur:2050 Artificial Biology " [Venture City]. ...bit creepy, but don't let that stop you !  Cheers man ! 🧭🍀✨️🧭

author: @Da5idc
text:
Excellent! I have done cyanotypes the usual way and this is a novel method
Now, all I need is a resin printer
A further thought - I saw a video about printing on leaves - basically it is putting a transparency on a young leaf so the exposed parts will lighten (or darken, I can't remember the details)
I wonder if the UV light will work with this idea

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: very likely mate and i am glad you liked the video - oh by the way you could just buy the UV light

replies.author: @Da5idc
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering  I know, but it's funnier saying I need a printer 😁

author: @stewartpalmer2456
text: You just made me think. Shame on you. You can probably do the same thing in Blender after importing a GIMP inverted image. Just save it as an STL. I've been learning height maps and Alpha images for creating 3D models in Blender. Just don't forget to check the normals before saving the STL's

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: shame on me? why shame - so blender does it too - awesome - there are always a million ways to accomplish any particular job - you should know that

replies.author: @stewartpalmer2456
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Because you made me think. I was joking sir.

author: @bobcalhoun3714
text: That's pretty cool. There are silk screen processes that use UV light to make the stencil that the ink passes through (EZScreen makes a kit.) The washing and curing station also uses a UV light? Print your image on a sheet of acetate, back that up with the coated paper, and you could use it to print a strip for a zoetrope? Oooh, does that mean you could paint a surface with the Elegoo plastic emulsion and cure it with UV?

author: @TupperWallace
text: John Herschel’s dad, William, was an organist and musician in Bath when he became a maker and self-taught astronomer. Unhappy with a purchased telescope, he learned how to grind lenses and went on to double the size of the observed solar system from his back garden by finding the planet Uranus. He discovered infrared light, leaving it to someone else to look for ultraviolet. His house is a wonderful  museum in a long row of identical honey-colored stone townhouses in Bath including his basement workshop and the cracked slate where he once spilled molten mirror metal. He is an inspiration to amateur inventors.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: he certainly is mate - thanks for taking the time to post that

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: Now I'm doing some thinking as to how it may work with making circuit boards. Would that black material be resistant to acid resist as used in etch copper on a pcb? One of the most common acids used is ferric chloride. Got any ideas on this? Is this even accurate enough considering that 0,008mm is often a minimum smallest spacing used in the pcb industry.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't know mate but deserves looking into

text: I like to spray my fully exposed cyanotypes with a light solution of hydrogen peroxide before the final rinse, it makes them more vibrant.

author: @lopsumtathro
text: hi mate, to dry the wet watercolor paper tape one edge down on a wooden board,then pull it tort and tape the opposite side and the same for the other 2 sides ,so as it dries it shrinks and gets stretched tight and flat , when totally dry will remain flat! thats how artists use that paper with water colors.

author: @lowrads
text: The opportunity cost for mounting a laser on a FDM printer seems to be nil, considering it can be used for axis monitoring in low power, and engraving at high power, making them multifunction devices.

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text:
Ah, amazing historical info. Thank you for making this video. BTW, I recommend Paint DOT net which is a free tool similar to paint, but simply much better. It isn't as complicated as "gimp" but that is also another paint type tool you can use.
This makes me wonder if this can be easily adapted to making PCB traces... OIC, that adamjo had the same question as well... yea,... interesting stuff.
The only thing about the "tea" method is that it sort of messes with the contrast of the white paper background. Does a rinse, afterward,  with Citric acid work?

author: @adamjo789
text:
I do wonder if it'd be possible to place a photoelectric material at the bottom of the printing bed, such that it generates a small charge where the UV interacts with the substrate, then discharge that through an electrolyte solution, therefore printing a thin metal object in the shape projected onto the substrate from the cations in the solution.
Like printing circuitry and microchips using electron lithography, but with more steps (and less money!)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i have no idea mate - but that is a fascinating suggestion - i think i will research this a bit - cheers

author: @totherarf
text:
Most of my working life I have been getting into trouble!
The reason is that even if there is a perfectly good way of doing things I look for alternatives. Those around me question why I would even try when obviously the best way is the way everybody does it. Even if my methods are not quite as good, I view them as a success because I did it differently!

I think there is a little of my mindset in you Rob. (yes that is a compliment ...... I think) ;o)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - i think there is mate!
